---
title: "AI in Economics Research"
author: "Graduate Economic History"
date: "Lukas Althoff"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    chakra: '../../shared/remark-latest.min.js'
    css: ['default', 'metropolis', 'metropolis-fonts', '../../shared/my-css.css']
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)

opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "100%"
)

deck_dir <- normalizePath(dirname(knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE)), winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
stanford_fig_dir <- file.path(deck_dir, "files", "stanford_survey_comparison")

system2(
  "python3",
  c(file.path(deck_dir, "generate_stanford_survey_figures.py")),
  stdout = TRUE,
  stderr = TRUE
)
```

class: agenda

# Agenda

<ul class="agenda-list">
<li class="current">AI in Science</li>
<li class="upcoming">AI at Stanford</li>
<li class="upcoming">Methods for Continuous Adaptation</li>
</ul>

---

# Why an economic historian studies AI

.center-content[
- Many AI questions are historical: how general-purpose technologies diffuse, who gains, who loses

- The long-run record (steam, electricity, computing) is the only at-scale evidence on these dynamics

- This course uses that record to discipline claims about AI

- This lecture: how AI is changing research, and a workflow to adapt
]

---

# Measuring AI adoption via OpenAlex

.center-content[
- [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/): open catalog of **250M+ scholarly works** with real-time metadata <a class="btn" href="#pub-volume">Publication volume</a>

- Classification: 5 domains → 26 fields → ~250 subfields → ~4,500 topics

- **AI adoption** = share of papers mentioning AI-related terms in title or abstract

- Tracks AI diffusion across all research fields, not just computer science
]

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-ai-mention-fields, out.width='82%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "ai_mention_fields.png"))
```
]

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-ai-mention-econ-subfields, out.width='82%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "ai_mention_econ_subfields.png"))
```
]

---

# Top-cited examples from the AI-classified set

<div style="min-height: 68%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center;">
<div style="font-size: 58%; margin-top: 0.2em;">
<div style="display: flex; gap: 1rem; align-items: flex-start;">

<div style="flex: 1;">
<div class="hi" style="margin-bottom: 0.5em;">Industrial Organization</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
1. "Why Do Management Practices Differ across Firms and Countries?"<br/>
<span class="gray">Bloom &amp; Van Reenen (2010)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
2. "Information Technology, Workplace Organization and the Demand for Skilled Labor: Firm-Level Evidence"<br/>
<span class="gray">Bresnahan, Brynjolfsson &amp; Hitt (1999)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
3. "Artificial intelligence, firm growth, and product innovation"<br/>
<span class="gray">Babina et al. (2023)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
4. "Robots and Jobs: Evidence from US Labor Markets"<br/>
<span class="gray">Acemoglu &amp; Restrepo (2017)</span>
</div>

<div style="line-height: 1.15;">
5. "Artificial Intelligence and Jobs: Evidence from Online Vacancies"<br/>
<span class="gray">Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell &amp; Restrepo (2022)</span>
</div>
</div>

<div style="flex: 1;">
<div class="hi" style="margin-bottom: 0.5em;">Macroeconomics</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
1. "Modified SEIR and AI prediction of the epidemics trend of COVID-19 in China under public health interventions"<br/>
<span class="gray">Yang et al. (2020)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
2. "Ranking the effectiveness of worldwide COVID-19 government interventions"<br/>
<span class="gray">Haug et al. (2020)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
3. "Comparing SARS-CoV-2 with SARS-CoV and influenza pandemics"<br/>
<span class="gray">Petersen et al. (2020)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
4. "Impact of COVID-19 pandemic on information management research and practice: Transforming education, work and life"<br/>
<span class="gray">Dwivedi et al. (2020)</span>
</div>

<div style="line-height: 1.15;">
5. "Artificial intelligence in sustainable energy industry: Status Quo, challenges and opportunities"<br/>
<span class="gray">Ahmad et al. (2021)</span>
</div>
</div>

<div style="flex: 1;">
<div class="hi" style="margin-bottom: 0.5em;">Economic History</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
1. "General Purpose Technologies"<br/>
<span class="gray">Jovanovic &amp; Rousseau (2005)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
2. "Measuring Central Bank Communication: An Automated Approach with Application to FOMC Statements"<br/>
<span class="gray">Lucca &amp; Trebbi (2009)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
3. "Increasing differences between firms: market power and the macro-economy"<br/>
<span class="gray">Van Reenen (2018)</span>
</div>

<div style="margin-bottom: 0.55em; line-height: 1.15;">
4. "Data and the Aggregate Economy"<br/>
<span class="gray">Veldkamp &amp; Chung (2024)</span>
</div>

<div style="line-height: 1.15;">
5. "Invariant Causal Prediction for Sequential Data"<br/>
<span class="gray">Pfister, Bühlmann &amp; Peters (2018)</span>
</div>
</div>

</div>
</div>
</div>

---
class: agenda

# Agenda

<ul class="agenda-list">
<li class="done">AI in Science</li>
<li class="current">AI at Stanford</li>
<li class="upcoming">Methods for Continuous Adaptation</li>
</ul>

---

class: inverse, middle

# AI at Stanford

- This is a selected sample, not a representative Stanford cross-section
- Students in this class
- Others at Stanford (excluding this class; selectively invited respondents in different disciplines)

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-stanford-time-saved, out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(stanford_fig_dir, "stanford_vs_recent_ai_time_saved_total.png"))
```
]

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-stanford-frequency, out.width='92%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(stanford_fig_dir, "stanford_vs_recent_ai_frequency.png"))
```
]

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-stanford-tools, out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(stanford_fig_dir, "stanford_vs_recent_ai_tools.png"))
```
]

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-stanford-active-hours, out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(stanford_fig_dir, "stanford_vs_recent_ai_active_hours.png"))
```
]

---
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-stanford-autonomy, out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(stanford_fig_dir, "stanford_vs_recent_ai_autonomy_self.png"))
```
]

---
class: agenda

# Agenda

<ul class="agenda-list">
<li class="done">AI in Science</li>
<li class="done">AI at Stanford</li>
<li class="current">Methods for Continuous Adaptation</li>
</ul>

---

# Using LLMs to generate data

.center-content[
- Turn unstructured sources into structured data: scanned documents, images, maps, free text

- Course examples:
  - Task-level skill and AI-exposure measures from job text <span class="gray">(Althoff & Reichardt 2026)</span>
  - Belief in progress from digitized British books <span class="gray">(Almelhem et al. 2025)</span>
  - Land use from satellite imagery <span class="gray">(Dippel, Frye & Leonard 2025)</span>

- Workflow: define a schema, extract with a vision or language model, validate against a hand-coded sample

- Always verify: spot-check accuracy and report error rates
]

---

name: strategy
# A strategy for continuous adaptation

.center-content[
1. Experiment with AI tools (= investment)

  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-cursor-1">Cursor setup</a>
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-best-practices">Best practices</a>

2. Keep trying new models
  
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-arena-ai">Arena AI</a>

3. Exchanging tacit knowledge
  
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-pair-programming">Pair programming</a>

4. Set up an AI-native knowledge base

  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-obsidian">Obsidian</a>

5. Codify your tacit knowledge and workflows

  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-llm-skills-intro">Skills portable across LLMs</a>
]

---
class: inverse, middle

# Appendix

---
name: appendix-cursor-1
class: middle

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

.center-content[
```{r fig-cursor-1, out.width='85%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "adaptation", "cursor1.png"))
```
]

---
name: appendix-cursor-2
class: middle

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

.center-content[
```{r fig-cursor-2, out.width='85%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "adaptation", "cursor2.png"))
```
]

---
name: appendix-cursor-3
class: middle

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

.center-content[
```{r fig-cursor-3, out.width='85%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "adaptation", "cursor3.png"))
```
]

---
name: appendix-best-practices

# Some best practices

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

- Choosing a mode and model
  - Use **cheap models** in **agent mode** for easy tasks (*Composer 2*)  
  - Use **advanced models** in **planning mode** for complex tasks (*Opus 4.6*, *GPT-5.4*, etc.)
  - Personally, I like planning with *Claude Opus 4.6* and executing via *GPT-5.4*

- Cursor vs. Cursor Extensions
  - Most models available through **Cursor chat**
  - **Extensions** for *Claude Code* and *OpenAI Codex* within Cursor
  - Extensions valuable because **"harness"** is different and designed for longer-running tasks

- Try using voice instead of typing (Wispr Flow, Cursor's own, etc.)

- Create an AI-native **knowledge base** using *Obsidian*

- Codify your tacit knowledge and workflows as **skills** (portable across LLMs)

- Try out new models regularly (e.g., Arena AI)

---
name: appendix-llm-skills-intro

# What LLM skills are

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;">
  <a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a>
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-llm-skills-map">Skill map</a>
</div>

.center-content[
- **Skills** are reusable instructions for recurring tasks you want an LLM to handle in a disciplined way

- They encode tacit knowledge into a portable workflow: what to do, in what order, what standards to apply, and when to use tools

- Good skills reduce repeated prompting, make outputs more consistent, and let you reuse the same workflow across projects and models

- You can build skills for literature reviews, coding, data cleaning, slide design, referee responses, or research ideation

- Concretely, a skill is often just a Markdown or text file with instructions, written much like you would write instructions for a human, that the large language model reads to understand what it is supposed to do
]

---
name: appendix-llm-skills-map

# Skills Across the Research Process

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;">
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-llm-skills-intro">Back</a>
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-llm-skills">Ideation example</a>
</div>

.pull-left[
- **Ideation**
  - `brainstorming`

- **Data gathering**
  - `data-gathering`
  - `web-scraping`
  - `digitization`

- **Data analysis**

- **Writing**
  - `writing`
  - `style`, `structure`, `formatting`
  - `presentations`, `first-draft`
]

.pull-right[
- **Reading**

- **Reviewing**

- **Mentorship**

- **Administration**
]

---
name: appendix-llm-skills

# Skill example: ideation

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;">
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-llm-skills-map">Back</a>
  <a class="btn" href="#strategy">Strategy</a>
</div>

.center-content[
- **Input**: a project idea, draft, or short summary

- <span class="hi-laorange">Main agent</span>: writes a structured summary that serves as memory for <span class="hi-layellow">sub-agents</span>

- <span class="hi-laorange">Main agent</span> asks <span class="hi-layellow">sub-agents</span>:
  - Recommendations on framing, empirical strategy, data, heterogeneity, theory, wild cards

- <span class="hi-layellow">Sub-agents</span>: work in parallel
  - Each approaches question from different angle (expert personas are described to the agent)
  - Each returns concrete ideas

- <span class="hi-laorange">Main agent</span>: aggregates and rates the output
  - Clusters overlap and looks for convergence across <span class="hi-layellow">sub-agents</span>
  - Combined score = transformative potential × feasibility, plus a convergence, adjusted for novelty

- **Output**: a short synthesis plus a ranked shortlist of next directions
]

---
name: appendix-obsidian

# How the Obsidian knowledge base works

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;">
  <a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a>
  <a class="btn" href="#appendix-obsidian-image">Vault screenshot</a>
</div>

.center-content[
- **Folder flow**: `raw/` stores source material, `wiki/summaries/` creates one note per source, `wiki/concepts/` distills reusable ideas, and `wiki/index.md` gives one-line navigation across the whole vault

- **LLM workflow**: `.cursorrules` tells Cursor to read new raw files, write summaries, extract concepts, update related notes, and always refresh the master index

- **Automatic cross-referencing**: Obsidian `[[wikilinks]]`, related frontmatter, and backlinks turn each note into a node in a graph rather than a standalone file

- **Fast retrieval**: the index keeps one sentence per item, so the LLM can cheaply scan the whole knowledge base first, then open only the few notes that matter

- **Compounding through interaction**: when a chat produces a useful synthesis, clarification, or new connection, ask the LLM to file it back into the vault, so the knowledge base grows with your own understanding
]

---
name: appendix-obsidian-image
class: middle

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

.center-content[
```{r fig-obsidian, out.width='85%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "adaptation", "obsidian.png"))
```
]

---
name: appendix-arena-ai
class: middle

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

.center-content[
```{r fig-arena-ai, out.width='85%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "adaptation", "arena_ai.png"))
```
]

---
name: appendix-pair-programming
class: middle

<div style="position: absolute; bottom: 20px; left: 30px; z-index: 1000;"><a class="btn" href="#strategy">Back</a></div>

.center-content[
```{r fig-pair-programming, out.width='68%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "adaptation", "pair_programming.png"))
```
]

---
name: pub-volume
class: middle

.center-content[
```{r fig-papers-by-domain, out.width='88%'}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(deck_dir, "files", "papers_by_domain.png"))
```
]

<a class="btn btn-back" href="#3">← Back</a>
